\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}

\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}

Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
\[
  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
  \qquad
  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
\]
is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of invertible sheaves
(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.

% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
% groups)
% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
% SOURCE QUOTE:
% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
% abelian groups defined by the formula
% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
% \end{dfn}
%  [\dots]
% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
% topologies  by
% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
%  \end{dfn}"
\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
homomorphisms.}

Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
products and the structure sheaf, so the image
\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
structure. This is the content of
\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.

\subsection*{The critical path: \texorpdfstring{\(\otimes\)}{tensor}-invertibility,
not geometric local triviality}

The mathematics is standard; the formalisation question is which Lean
\emph{predicate} carries the notion of ``line bundle'' so that the group law
costs the least. We use the \(\otimes\)-\emph{invertibility} predicate, exactly
as Mathlib's commutative-ring Picard group does
(\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), in
\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}): an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
is one possessing a \(\otimes\)-inverse,
\[
  \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
    \exists\, N,\;\; M \otimes_X N \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_X .
\]
This is the equivalent characterisation of an invertible sheaf in
Stacks tag 01CR (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} below).

Under this predicate the group law lives on \emph{isomorphism classes}, where
each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting the existence of an
isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)), insensitive to which
isomorphism witnesses it. The coherence data of a monoidal category --- the
pentagon, triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure
--- is therefore \emph{never consumed}. What the law genuinely requires is only:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item a binary tensor-product operation
    \(\otimes_X : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
    isomorphism classes (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj},
    \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality},
    \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobjisoofiso});
  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object for
    \(\otimes_X\);
  \item the \emph{monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\)} ---
    associator, left/right unitors and braiding --- as bare
    \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\)-isomorphisms
    (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
           lem:tensorobj_right_unitor,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso});
  \item the predicate \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of
    \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, which \emph{carries the inverse
    existentially}: the dual / tensor-inverse is part of the datum of being
    invertible, so no separate ``\(L \otimes L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)''
    construction is on the critical path.
\end{enumerate}
The coherence isomorphisms of (3) are built by the cheap pattern
\(\mathtt{sheafification}.\mathtt{mapIso}\) of the corresponding
presheaf-of-modules coherence isomorphism
(\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\): \(\alpha_\bullet\), \(\lambda_\bullet\),
\(\rho_\bullet\), \(\beta_\bullet\)) --- exactly the shape of the already-built
\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_unit\_iso}\) and \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\). No abstract
presheaf pullback, no \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) left adjoint, and
no comparison of a sheafified \(\otimes\) against a local restriction enters.
In particular the geometric local-triviality route --- the predicate
\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \equiv \forall x,\ \exists\ \text{affine}\
U \ni x,\ M|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), together with the
restriction-compatibility isomorphism
\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) it forces --- is \emph{not} on the critical
path; it is recorded as an optional, off-path bridge
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,%
        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) connected to \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) by an
equivalence that the group law never uses
(\cref{rem:islocallytrivial_off_path}). The full symmetric monoidal category on
\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is likewise not required; see
\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.

\section{Mathlib API survey}
\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}

The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
(\texttt{b80f227}) split across three layers.

\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
sectionwise tensor product
\[
  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
\]
for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, \emph{together with the
associator \(\alpha_\bullet\), the left/right unitors \(\lambda_\bullet,
\rho_\bullet\), and the braiding \(\beta_\bullet\)} making the resulting
category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules), and
the coherence isomorphisms it ships are the raw material that the
scheme-level coherence isomorphisms of \cref{sec:tensorobj_coherence} are
transported from.

\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), the sheafification functor on the
small Zariski site, and the structure-sheaf constant module
\(\mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\). It
does \emph{not} ship a binary tensor-product operation
\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.

\paragraph{Layer 3: the commutative-ring Picard group (the design template).}
Mathlib's \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} carries, over a fixed
commutative ring \(R\), the predicate \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) and the
Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R = \mathrm{Units}\bigl(
\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) with its
\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\) commutative-group instance. There the
commutative-group law is \emph{transported from the units of the commutative
monoid of \(\otimes\)-isomorphism classes} of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R\): the
genuine \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance on \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R\) makes
\(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\) a commutative monoid, and the
Picard group is its group of units, an element being a unit iff the underlying
module is \(\otimes\)-invertible. This module is the \emph{design template} for
the present chapter: we reproduce the same units-of-iso-class-monoid shape one
layer up, at the scheme level, where the monoidal data is supplied by
\(\otimes_X\) and its coherence isomorphisms instead of an ambient
\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance. (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} itself
records ``connect to invertible sheaves'' as an open task, so the scheme-level
Picard group is genuinely absent from Mathlib and supplied project-side here.)

\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
Layer 2 by sheafification, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The data the group law needs on top
of the bare operation are the four \emph{coherence isomorphisms} (associator,
left and right unitor, braiding) and the \emph{invertibility predicate}
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrying the inverse. The coherence isomorphisms are
each obtained by applying the sheafification functor's \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) to
the corresponding presheaf-of-modules coherence isomorphism of Layer 1,
followed --- for the unitors --- by the sheafification-adjunction counit on the
already-sheaf unit, in exact analogy with the existing
\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_unit\_iso}\) (which is the \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X
\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) special case of this pattern). The project
does \emph{not} require a global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance on all
quasi-coherent \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\)
structure: monoidal coherence \emph{equations} are never consumed by a group law
on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The substrate is
built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home) and
the consumer instance is closed against it; a parallel Mathlib upstream PR with
the same content is welcome but not on the critical path.

\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}

\begin{definition}

\leanok
[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
  \[
    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
  \]
  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
  canonical compatibility
  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
     \;\cong\;
     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.

  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
  \[
    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
  \]
  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
  the datum the present lemma provides; the coherence isomorphisms
  (associator, unitors, braiding) are constructed separately in
  \cref{sec:tensorobj_coherence}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
  sheafification.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Transport of isomorphisms through $\otimes_X$]
  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobjisoofiso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoOfIso}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme. Given isomorphisms \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\)
  and \(e' : N \xrightarrow{\sim} N'\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\),
  there is an induced isomorphism
  \[
    M \otimes_X N \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\; M' \otimes_X N'
  \]
  obtained by applying the sheafification functor's \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) to the
  presheaf-of-modules tensor of the underlying isomorphisms of \(e\) and \(e'\).
  Consequently the operation \(\otimes_X\) descends to a well-defined binary
  operation on \emph{isomorphism classes} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\):
  \([M] \otimes [N] := [M \otimes_X N]\) is independent of the chosen
  representatives.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
  The morphisms \(e, e'\) are sent by the forgetful functor
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.forget}\) to isomorphisms of presheaves of modules;
  the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of two isomorphisms is an
  isomorphism, and the sheafification functor's \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) carries it to
  an isomorphism \(M \otimes_X N \cong M' \otimes_X N'\). Well-definedness on
  isomorphism classes is immediate: if \(M \cong M'\) and \(N \cong N'\) then
  \(M \otimes_X N \cong M' \otimes_X N'\), so the class
  \([M \otimes_X N]\) depends only on \([M]\) and \([N]\).
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}
[The \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)]
  \label{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
  % modules), Definition 01CS (definition-invertible) + Lemma 0B8K
  % (lemma-invertible, the "$\exists\,\mathcal{N}$" characterisation)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4046-L4079).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{definition}
  % \label{definition-invertible}
  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. An
  % {\it invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module} is a sheaf
  % of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{L}$ such that
  % the functor
  % $$
  % \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X),\quad
  % \mathcal{F} \longmapsto \mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{F}
  % $$
  % is an equivalence of categories. We say that $\mathcal{L}$ is
  % {\it trivial} if it is isomorphic as an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
  % to $\mathcal{O}_X$.
  % \end{definition}
  % [\dots]
  % \begin{lemma}
  % \label{lemma-invertible}
  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Let $\mathcal{L}$
  % be an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. Equivalent are
  % \begin{enumerate}
  % \item $\mathcal{L}$ is invertible, and
  % \item there exists an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{N}$
  % such that
  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$.
  % \end{enumerate}
  % In this case $\mathcal{L}$ is locally a direct summand of a finite free
  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-module and the module $\mathcal{N}$ in (2) is isomorphic to
  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
  % \end{lemma}"
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CS and Lemma~0B8K. An invertible
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is one for which \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
  (-)\) is an equivalence; equivalently there exists \(\mathcal{N}\) with
  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and
  then \(\mathcal{N} \cong \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
  \mathcal{O}_X)\).}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Define the
  proposition
  \[
    \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \;\;:\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
      \exists\, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X,\;\;
        \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_X\bigr),
  \]
  where \(\mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
  is the designated unit object for \(\otimes_X\). This is the scheme-level
  analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) and the second clause of
  Stacks Lemma~0B8K. The predicate \emph{carries the inverse existentially}: the
  witness \(N\) (necessarily isomorphic to the dual
  \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X)\) by Lemma~0B8K) is part of
  the datum of being invertible. Consequently no standalone
  ``\(M \otimes_X M^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)'' construction is required on the
  critical path: invertibility \emph{is} the existence of a tensor inverse.
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}
[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
  depend on it.

  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
  classes} of invertible modules, and every group axiom there is a
  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
  coherence \emph{equations} of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle,
  hexagon, and a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- are never consumed
  by the group law; only the coherence \emph{isomorphisms} as bare isos are
  used. Building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all quasi-coherent modules
  would route through the localization-monoidal transport
  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
  buys coherence the group law never uses.

  The group law needs only the coherence isomorphisms of
  \cref{sec:tensorobj_coherence} together with the invertibility predicate
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}. We therefore record the global
  monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the critical path}. This mirrors
  Mathlib's own Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\)
  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is
  transported from \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)
  \bigr)\) over a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and
  no \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
\end{remark}

\section{The monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\)}
\label{sec:tensorobj_coherence}

This section records the coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\) as bare
\(\mathrm{Nonempty}\)-isomorphisms. Each is the scheme-level transport of the
corresponding presheaf-of-modules coherence isomorphism of Layer 1
(\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) under the sheafification functor, in the
pattern already validated by \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_unit\_iso}\) (the
\(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) special case). No
coherence \emph{equation} is asserted: only the existence of each isomorphism is
claimed, which is all the group law on iso-classes consumes
(\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).

\begin{lemma}
[Unit \(\otimes_X\)-iso \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \cong
  \mathcal{O}_X\)]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_unit_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_unit_iso}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and
  \(\mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\). Then
  \[
    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_X .
  \]
  This is the prototype of the cheap transport pattern: it is built by applying
  the sheafification functor's \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) to the presheaf-level left
  unitor \(\lambda_{\mathbf 1}\) (the monoidal unit of
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) being exactly \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathtt{val}\)),
  composed with the sheafification-adjunction counit isomorphism on the
  already-sheaf unit. The general coherence isomorphisms below copy this shape.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  By \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj},
  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X\) is the sheafification of the
  presheaf-of-modules tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathtt{val}\) with
  itself. The monoidal unit of the presheaf-of-modules category is
  \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathtt{val}\), so the presheaf left unitor
  \(\lambda_{\mathbf 1} : \mathbf 1 \otimes \mathbf 1 \xrightarrow{\sim}
  \mathbf 1\) is available; \(\mathtt{sheafification}.\mathtt{mapIso}\,
  \lambda_{\mathbf 1}\) yields
  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \cong
    \mathtt{sheafification}(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathtt{val})\), and the
  sheafification-adjunction counit on the already-sheaf object
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) is an isomorphism
  \(\mathtt{sheafification}(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathtt{val}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\).
  Composing gives the claim.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Left unitor of \(\otimes_X\)]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_unit_unit_iso}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) arbitrary.
  Then there is an isomorphism
  \[
    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\cong\; M
  \]
  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); equivalently
  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \cong M)\) holds. No
  local-triviality or rank hypothesis on \(M\) is used.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_unit_unit_iso}
  The monoidal unit of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is
  \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathtt{val}\), so the presheaf-level left unitor
  \(\lambda_{M.\mathtt{val}} : \mathbf 1 \otimes M.\mathtt{val}
  \xrightarrow{\sim} M.\mathtt{val}\) of Layer 1 is available. Applying the
  sheafification functor's \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) gives
  \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M = \mathtt{sheafification}(\mathbf 1 \otimes
  M.\mathtt{val}) \cong \mathtt{sheafification}(M.\mathtt{val})\). Since \(M\) is
  already a sheaf, the sheafification-adjunction counit
  \(\mathtt{sheafification}(M.\mathtt{val}) \cong M\) is an isomorphism;
  composing yields \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \cong M\). This is exactly the
  shape of \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_unit_iso} with the second factor general in
  place of \(\mathcal{O}_X\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Right unitor of \(\otimes_X\)]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_right_unitor}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_unit_unit_iso}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) arbitrary.
  Then there is an isomorphism
  \[
    M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\cong\; M
  \]
  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); equivalently
  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \cong M)\) holds.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_unit_unit_iso}
  Identical to \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} with the presheaf-level
  \emph{right} unitor \(\rho_{M.\mathtt{val}} : M.\mathtt{val} \otimes \mathbf 1
  \xrightarrow{\sim} M.\mathtt{val}\) in place of the left unitor:
  \(\mathtt{sheafification}.\mathtt{mapIso}\,\rho_{M.\mathtt{val}}\) followed by
  the sheafification counit on the sheaf \(M\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Braiding / symmetry of \(\otimes_X\)]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_braiding}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) arbitrary.
  Then there is an isomorphism
  \[
    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\; N \otimes_X M
  \]
  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); equivalently
  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(M \otimes_X N \cong N \otimes_X M)\) holds.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Both sides are sheafifications of presheaf-of-modules tensor products of the
  same two underlying presheaves in the two orders. The presheaf-of-modules
  braiding
  \(\beta_{M.\mathtt{val}, N.\mathtt{val}} : M.\mathtt{val} \otimes N.\mathtt{val}
  \xrightarrow{\sim} N.\mathtt{val} \otimes M.\mathtt{val}\) of Layer 1 is an
  isomorphism; \(\mathtt{sheafification}.\mathtt{mapIso}\,
  \beta_{M.\mathtt{val}, N.\mathtt{val}}\) carries it directly to
  \(M \otimes_X N \cong N \otimes_X M\). No counit step and no
  nested-sheafification absorption is needed: neither side nests a
  sheafification inside the presheaf tensor.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Associator of \(\otimes_X\)]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  % NOTE: Unlike the unitors and the braiding, the associator nests a
  % sheafification inside the presheaf tensor on BOTH sides
  % (tensorObj (tensorObj M N) P = sheafification(ptensor(sheafification(...).val,
  % P.val))). Transporting the presheaf associator alpha thus additionally
  % requires the "sheafification--tensor absorption" isomorphism
  % sheafification(ptensor(sheafification(A).val, B)) ~= sheafification(ptensor(A, B))
  % (and its mirror), i.e. that the sheafification-localizing morphism property is
  % stable under whiskering by an arbitrary module. This is the SAME whiskering
  % stability that rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path flags as routing through
  % the absent MonoidalClosed(PresheafOfModules R). The associator is therefore
  % the one coherence iso that is NOT the ~15-LOC cheap pattern; see the
  % blueprint-writer report's "Strategy-modifying findings" for the plan agent.
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
  arbitrary. Then there is an isomorphism
  \[
    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \;\cong\; M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
  \]
  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); equivalently
  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong
  M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P))\) holds. Only the existence of the isomorphism is
  asserted; no pentagon identity is claimed or needed.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  Write \(\mathtt{sh}\) for the sheafification functor and \(\otimes^{p}\) for
  the presheaf-of-modules tensor of Layer 1. By
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj},
  \[
    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
      \;=\; \mathtt{sh}\bigl(\,
        \mathtt{sh}(M.\mathtt{val} \otimes^{p} N.\mathtt{val}).\mathtt{val}
        \;\otimes^{p}\; P.\mathtt{val}\,\bigr),
    \qquad
    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
      \;=\; \mathtt{sh}\bigl(\,
        M.\mathtt{val} \;\otimes^{p}\;
        \mathtt{sh}(N.\mathtt{val} \otimes^{p} P.\mathtt{val}).\mathtt{val}\,
        \bigr).
  \]
  The presheaf-level associator
  \(\alpha_{M.\mathtt{val},\,N.\mathtt{val},\,P.\mathtt{val}}\) of Layer 1 gives
  an isomorphism
  \((M.\mathtt{val} \otimes^{p} N.\mathtt{val}) \otimes^{p} P.\mathtt{val}
    \cong M.\mathtt{val} \otimes^{p} (N.\mathtt{val} \otimes^{p} P.\mathtt{val})\),
  and \(\mathtt{sh}.\mathtt{mapIso}\) transports it. To connect this to the two
  displayed objects one additionally uses the \emph{sheafification--tensor
  absorption} isomorphism: the sheafification-unit
  \(\eta_A : A \to \mathtt{sh}(A).\mathtt{val}\) becomes an isomorphism after
  tensoring with an arbitrary presheaf \(B\) and re-sheafifying,
  \[
    \mathtt{sh}\bigl(\mathtt{sh}(A).\mathtt{val} \otimes^{p} B\bigr)
      \;\cong\; \mathtt{sh}\bigl(A \otimes^{p} B\bigr),
  \]
  applied with \((A, B) = (M.\mathtt{val} \otimes^{p} N.\mathtt{val},\,
  P.\mathtt{val})\) on the left and the mirror version on the right. Composing
  the two absorption isomorphisms with \(\mathtt{sh}.\mathtt{mapIso}\,\alpha\)
  yields the asserted isomorphism. As recorded in the
  \(\mathtt{NOTE}\) above and in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}, the
  absorption isomorphism is the whiskering-stability of the sheafification
  localizer for an arbitrary module, the one ingredient here that is not the
  bare \(\mathtt{sh}.\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern of the unitors and braiding.
\end{proof}

\section{Off-critical-path supplements}
\label{sec:tensorobj_offpath}

The lemmas in this section are retained for potential reuse and for the eventual
comparison of \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) with the geometric local-triviality
predicate. \emph{None of them is consumed by the relative Picard group law},
which rests entirely on \cref{sec:tensorobj_coherence} and
\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}.

\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  % NOTE: off the critical path. A self-contained CommRingCat-level
  % lax-monoidal supplement; NOT used by the group law.
  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
  \[
    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
      \;\;:=\;\;
    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
  \]
  is lax monoidal. This lemma is off the critical path for the tensor group law.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\)). The
  presheaf monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes
  M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\), and the presheaf functor
  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts sectionwise as
  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\). Hence the
  lax structure maps \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) assemble from the per-section
  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones; packaging these as a
  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure. The
  composite is lax monoidal by \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\). The
  commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  % NOTE: OFF the critical path for the group law. With the IsInvertible
  % predicate (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible) carrying the inverse and the
  % coherence isos of sec:tensorobj_coherence supplying associativity/unit/
  % commutativity, the group law NO LONGER needs this restriction-compatibility
  % isomorphism (which belonged to the abandoned geometric local-triviality
  % route). It is retained only as the optional bridge toward the geometric
  % predicate (rem:islocallytrivial_off_path). Its full proof bottoms out in the
  % opaque PresheafOfModules.pullback abstract left adjoint, which has NO
  % sectionwise formula; the genuine residual (a presheaf-level pushforward-vs-
  % pullback comparison plus a strong-monoidal restrictScalars along the ring iso
  % f.appIso) is ~200-300 LOC of absent Mathlib infrastructure and is NOT
  % attempted on the critical path.
  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
  morphism
  \[
    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
  \]
  is an isomorphism. The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction
  along \(f\) is base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism}
  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), not along a general ring map --- and base change along a
  ring isomorphism is invertible and commutes with tensor products.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  This statement is true and natural, but its formal route is not on the group
  law's critical path and is recorded here only for completeness. Restriction
  along the open immersion \(f\) is naturally isomorphic to the abstract
  presheaf-of-modules pullback
  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\) along the
  open-immersion ring morphism \(\varphi\); the substrate \(\otimes_X\)
  sheafifies the presheaf tensor and the pullback commutes with sheafification.
  The residual presheaf-level comparison would be discharged sectionwise via the
  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\) and
  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalarsEquivalenceOfRingEquiv}\); however
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) is the opaque left adjoint of
  pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib, so the comparison
  bottoms out in two project-side, Mathlib-absent ingredients (a presheaf-level
  pushforward-versus-pullback comparison and a strong-monoidal
  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). As the group law no
  longer consumes this isomorphism, that residual is left unbuilt.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
  % NOTE: off the critical path. Part of the optional IsLocallyTrivial-to-
  % IsInvertible bridge (rem:islocallytrivial_off_path), not used by the group
  % law.
  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
  one.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. The tensor product of two
  free rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one; hence
  \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\), so \(L \otimes_X L'\) is
  locally trivial of rank one.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Dual of an invertible module is a tensor inverse]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
  % NOTE: off the critical path. The IsInvertible predicate
  % (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible) already carries the inverse existentially,
  % so this explicit-dual construction is NOT needed for the group law. Retained
  % for the geometric bridge and for identifying the inverse with the dual
  % (Stacks Lemma 0B8K / lemma-constructions-invertible).
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be a line
  bundle. The dual
  \(L^{-1} := \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\)
  is again a line bundle, and the evaluation map
  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\) is an isomorphism.
  Consequently every line bundle is \(\otimes_X\)-invertible in the sense of
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
  On a common affine trivialising cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is
  again free of rank one, and the evaluation map specialises to the contraction
  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
  isomorphism; hence the evaluation map is a global isomorphism. The witness
  \(L^{-1}\) then satisfies \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), so
  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,L\) holds. (This is Stacks
  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}.)
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
[\(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) versus \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\): off-path
  equivalence]
  \label{rem:islocallytrivial_off_path}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
  The project retains the geometric predicate
  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \equiv \forall x,\ \exists\ \text{affine}\
  U \ni x,\ M|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (the local-triviality stand-in used in
  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}) as a \emph{separate} predicate from
  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}. The two
  agree, but only one direction is non-trivial and \emph{neither direction is on
  the critical path}:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsInvertible}\) is
      \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}: a line bundle has its dual as a
      tensor inverse (Stacks Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}).
    \item \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Rightarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is the
      geometric content of Stacks Lemma~0B8M (an invertible module on a scheme,
      whose stalks are local rings, is locally free of rank one); it is the
      genuinely non-trivial direction and is needed nowhere in the group-law
      construction.
  \end{itemize}
  Because the group law is built on \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) directly
  (\cref{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}), this equivalence may be
  supplied later, independently, without blocking the abelian-group instance.
\end{remark}

\section{The commutative monoid of \(\otimes_X\)-iso-classes and the Picard group}
\label{sec:tensorobj_picmonoid}

\begin{proposition}
[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes_X\)-isomorphism classes; its units form
  \(\Pic(X)\)]
  \label{prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorClassCommMonoid}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobjisoofiso,
        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
        lem:tensorobj_right_unitor,
        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
  % modules), Definition 01CX (definition-pic) + Lemma lemma-constructions-
  % invertible (tensor of invertibles is invertible; dual is the inverse)
  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200-L4213 and L4350-L4357).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{lemma}
  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
  % \begin{enumerate}
  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$, $\mathcal{N}$ are invertible
  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, then so is
  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N}$.
  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
  % is an isomorphism.
  % \end{enumerate}
  % \end{lemma}
  % [\dots]
  % \begin{definition}
  % \label{definition-pic}
  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
  % corresponding to tensor product.
  % \end{definition}"
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; tensor product of invertibles is
  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of objects of
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) under \(\otimes_X\) form a \emph{commutative
  monoid}: multiplication \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\) is well-defined by
  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobjisoofiso}, the unit is the class
  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\) of the structure sheaf, and associativity, unitality and
  commutativity hold as equalities of isomorphism classes by the coherence
  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
  lem:tensorobj_right_unitor,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} (each a
  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\)-isomorphism, hence an equality of classes). An element
  \([M]\) is a \emph{unit} of this monoid if and only if \(M\) is
  \(\otimes_X\)-invertible in the sense of
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}: a tensor inverse of \([M]\) is exactly
  a class \([N]\) with \([M \otimes_X N] = [\mathcal{O}_X]\). The
  \emph{Picard group of \(X\)},
  \[
    \Pic(X) \;\;:=\;\; \mathrm{Units}\bigl(\text{monoid of }\otimes_X\text{-iso
      classes}\bigr),
  \]
  is therefore the abelian group of isomorphism classes of invertible modules
  under \(\otimes_X\), with unit \([\mathcal{O}_X]\) and inverse the class of the
  dual, exactly as in Stacks Definition~01CX. This reproduces, one layer up, the
  Mathlib design template \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and its
  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\): the commutative-group structure is the units of
  the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobjisoofiso,
        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
        lem:tensorobj_right_unitor,
        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
  Multiplication on iso-classes is well-defined by
  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobjisoofiso}. The commutative-monoid axioms are
  equalities of iso-classes, each witnessed by a coherence isomorphism:
  associativity by \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, left and right unitality by
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_right_unitor}, commutativity by
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. (Because the carrier is iso-classes, the monoid
  axioms are \emph{propositions} \([A]=[B]\) deduced from
  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(A \cong B)\); no coherence \emph{equation} between the
  isomorphisms is needed.) In any commutative monoid the units form an abelian
  group; by \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} the class \([M]\) is a unit
  precisely when \(M\) is \(\otimes_X\)-invertible, so the group of units is the
  group of iso-classes of invertible modules. By Stacks
  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} this group is closed under
  \(\otimes_X\) and dual, matching Stacks Definition~01CX's \(\Pic(X)\).
\end{proof}

\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}

The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works with a carrier
\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of invertible modules on the
fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the planned
re-instantiation of that carrier on the \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) predicate and
the interaction of \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) with the pullback functor
\(\pi_T^*\).

\begin{remark}
[\(\texttt{OnProduct}\) re-instantiated on \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)]
  \label{rem:onproduct_repoint}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid}
  The downstream carrier \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of
  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} is re-instantiated as the subtype
  \[
    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
    \;\;=\;\;
    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)
            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\},
  \]
  the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate of
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, in place of the geometric
  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) subtype. This realises the documented intent that
  \(\texttt{OnProduct}\) pair a carrier with an \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) witness,
  and aligns the carrier with Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)-based
  Picard group. Under this re-instantiation the abelian group on iso-classes of
  the subtype is exactly the units group of
  \cref{prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid} restricted to \(C \times_k T\); the
  geometric predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and
  connected by the off-path equivalence of \cref{rem:islocallytrivial_off_path}.
  (The re-instantiation lives in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}; this
  chapter only references it so its own statements are self-consistent.)
\end{remark}

\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Closure of \(\otimes_X\) on the invertible subtype]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid,
        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes. The bifunctor
  \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to the subtype of
  \(\otimes\)-invertible modules
  \[
    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\}
  \]
  (\cref{rem:onproduct_repoint}): if \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\) and
  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M'\) then \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes_{C
  \times_S T} M')\), the unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\) is invertible, and
  every invertible module has a tensor inverse on the subtype.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid}
  Closure under \(\otimes\) is the iso-class statement of
  \cref{prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid}(units closed under multiplication):
  if \(M \otimes N \cong \mathcal{O}\) and \(M' \otimes N' \cong \mathcal{O}\),
  then \((M \otimes M') \otimes (N \otimes N') \cong \mathcal{O}\) by the
  coherence isomorphisms of \cref{sec:tensorobj_coherence}, so
  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes M')\) with witness \(N \otimes N'\). The
  unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\) is invertible with witness itself
  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}). The tensor inverse is carried by the
  predicate by \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}. Thus the subtype is a
  submonoid all of whose elements are units --- an abelian group of iso-classes,
  the units group of \cref{prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid} on \(C \times_S T\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
  % NOTE: no Lean declaration yet.
  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
  functor
  \[
    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
  \]
  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} carries invertible modules to
  invertible modules and is compatible with \(\otimes\): there is a canonical
  natural isomorphism
  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
     \xrightarrow{\sim}
   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
  canonical isomorphism
  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
  Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules preserves the structure sheaf and tensor
  products: \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{O}_T \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\) and
  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'}) \cong \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}
  \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N'}\), the latter being the standard
  pullback-tensor compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
  (Stacks Lemma~\texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}). In particular pullback
  preserves \(\otimes\)-invertibility: if \(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N''}
  \cong \mathcal{O}_T\) then \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T}
  \pi_T^*\mathcal{N''} \cong \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), so
  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N})\) (this is the scheme-morphism case
  of Stacks Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}).
\end{proof}

\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}

The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_coherence,%
sec:tensorobj_picmonoid,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift} supplies all the data
missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean encoding. The consumer
instance below packages the abelian-group law
\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
relative Picard quotient as the units group of the \(\otimes\)-iso-class monoid,
descended through the \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) subgroup.

\begin{theorem}

\leanok
[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
  \uses{prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid,
        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}

  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
  % groups)
  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
  % abelian groups defined by the formula
  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
  % \end{dfn}
  %  [\dots]
  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
  % topologies  by
  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
  %  \end{dfn}"
  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
  \[
    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
  \]
  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
  abelian-group structure with addition
  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
  inverse
  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) is the substrate operation of
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} and \((-)^{-1}\) is the
  \(\otimes\)-inverse carried by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). With this structure the quotient
  map
  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid,
        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
  By \cref{prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid} applied to \(X := C \times_k T\),
  the isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible objects of
  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) --- i.e.\ of
  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\)
  (\cref{rem:onproduct_repoint,lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) --- form the
  abelian group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), the units of the commutative monoid of
  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes, with addition
  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\), neutral element
  \([\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\), and inverse the class of the
  \(\otimes\)-inverse carried by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\). No
  monoidal-coherence \emph{equation} is invoked: associativity, unit and
  commutativity are equalities of iso-classes from the coherence isomorphisms,
  and the inverse is part of the invertibility datum. This is exactly the
  Mathlib template \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\) (the units of
  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\)) transported to the scheme-level
  invertible subcategory.

  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries \(\otimes\) to
  \(\otimes\) and the unit to the unit and preserves invertibility, so the
  induced map on isomorphism classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k
  T)\) is a group homomorphism and its image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup.

  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The quotient
  of an abelian group by a subgroup is an abelian group, via the standard
  \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction (the relation
  \(L \sim L' \Leftrightarrow L \otimes_{C \times_k T} (L')^{-1} \in
  \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of the \(\mathtt{preimage\_subgroup}\) Setoid presupposes
  exactly the \(\otimes\) and \(\otimes\)-inverse supplied above), and the
  quotient map is a surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly the
  subgroup. Putting the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) and identifies it with the structure of
  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
\end{proof}

\section{LOC estimates and prover-lane sequencing}
\label{sec:tensorobj_loc_estimates}

The substrate splits into three logical pieces; each is one prover lane. The
route is deliberately \emph{lighter} than a monoidal-category build: no
pentagon/triangle/hexagon coherence \emph{equation} is discharged, no
\(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure is constructed
(\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}), and the abandoned
geometric-local-triviality route through \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\) is
\emph{not} on the critical path (\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).

\paragraph{Piece 1: definition + functoriality + iso-transport + invertibility
predicate
(\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,%
lem:scheme_modules_tensorobjisoofiso,def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}).}
The Lean target file is \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}.
The object \(\otimes_X\), the bifunctorial action, the iso-transport
\(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\) and the unit iso
\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_unit\_iso}\) are already formalised; the
\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) predicate is a one-line \(\mathrm{Prop}\)-valued
definition. Estimate: a few LOC project-side for the predicate on top of the
existing operation.

\paragraph{Piece 2: the coherence isomorphisms
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_right_unitor,%
lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}).}
The same file, building on Piece 1. The unitors and the braiding each copy the
validated \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_unit\_iso}\) pattern --- apply
\(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) to the presheaf-level
\(\lambda_\bullet\)/\(\rho_\bullet\)/\(\beta_\bullet\), followed (for the
unitors) by the sheafification-counit step --- at roughly \(15\) LOC each. The
associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}) additionally requires the
sheafification--tensor absorption isomorphism
\(\mathtt{sheafification}(\mathtt{sheafification}(A).\mathtt{val} \otimes^{p} B)
\cong \mathtt{sheafification}(A \otimes^{p} B)\) (whiskering-stability of the
sheafification localizer for an arbitrary module), which is \emph{not} the cheap
\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern; see \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path} and
the project's strategy notes. Estimate for the three cheap isos: \(\approx 45\)
LOC; the associator is flagged as the one piece that may need the absorption
lemma supplied first.

\paragraph{Piece 3: monoid/units + lift + consumer
(\cref{prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid,lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,%
lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}).}
The monoid-of-iso-classes and its units group
(\cref{prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid}) assemble the \(\mathtt{CommMonoid}\)
from Piece 2 and read off \(\Pic(X)\) as its units (mirroring
\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\)); the lift to the invertible subtype
(\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and the \(\pi_T^*\)-compatibility
(\cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}) restrict it to \(C \times_k T\);
the consumer instance
\(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup\_via\_tensorObj}\) lives
back in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean} and descends the
group via \texttt{QuotientAddGroup} on the \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) subgroup.
Estimate: \(\approx 30\)--\(50\) LOC project-side total.

\paragraph{Total + sequencing.} Roughly \(80\)--\(140\) LOC project-side ---
lighter than a monoidal-category route, since the coherence \emph{equation}
discharge and the \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) build are avoided entirely, and the
inverse is carried by the predicate. The recommended prover-lane sequence is
Piece 1 → Piece 2 → Piece 3, with the associator's absorption lemma scheduled
ahead of the consumer if it proves necessary. Once Piece 3 is established, the
dependent declarations \texttt{PicSharp}, \texttt{PicSharp.functorial},
\texttt{PicSharp.presheaf}, \texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf},
\texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf\_group\_structure} each close against
\cref{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}, following the patterns of chapter
\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, clearing the dependency of A.2.c
(\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) on chapter
\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.

\section{Out of scope}
\label{sec:tensorobj_out_of_scope}

The following are downstream of this chapter and live in dedicated
sibling chapters; this chapter does \emph{not} touch them:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{The sheafified relative Picard functor and its abelian-group target}
    (\cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification,
              thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}) --- once
    \cref{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj} is established, the
    \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued presheaf, its \'etale sheafification, and
    the sheafification preserving the abelian-group target close
    against it (chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}).
  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) by a scheme}
    (A.2.c, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) --- consumes the
    full \(\AddCommGroup\)-valued sheafified presheaf; representability is
    downstream of \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}.
  \item \textbf{Identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)} (A.3,
    \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}) --- once representability is
    in hand, the identity component is cut out by Hilbert polynomials.
  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property} (A.4,
    \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) --- consumes the full Route~A stack
    downstream of A.3.
  \item \textbf{The geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow
    \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) equivalence and \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\)}
    (\cref{rem:islocallytrivial_off_path,lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) --- retained
    as off-critical-path supplements; not required by the group law.
  \item \textbf{Mathlib upstream PR of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)} ---
    the substrate of this chapter is project-side, intentionally; a parallel
    Mathlib upstream PR is welcome but not on the critical path. Once the
    substrate ships in Mathlib, the \texttt{TensorObjSubstrate.lean} file can be
    slimmed to a single import + namespace re-export.
\end{itemize}

\section{Internal-consistency check}
\label{sec:tensorobj_consistency_check}

The Lean-pinned declaration blocks of this chapter form a connected
\texttt{\textbackslash uses\{\dots\}} chain whose leaves all resolve
either inside this chapter, inside chapter
\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b on disk), or inside
chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c on disk):
\begin{itemize}
  \item \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} introduces the substrate
    operation and depends on no prior block.
  \item \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} and
    \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobjisoofiso} use
    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}; the latter gives well-definedness on
    iso-classes.
  \item \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} uses
    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}; it is the \(\otimes\)-invertibility
    predicate carrying the inverse.
  \item \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path} records the full monoidal
    category as off the critical path.
  \item The coherence isomorphisms
    \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
          lem:tensorobj_right_unitor,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,%
          lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} use \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
    (the unitors additionally use \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_unit_iso}); all are
    bare \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\)-isomorphisms.
  \item The off-path supplements
    \cref{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal,lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,%
          lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,%
          lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} use
    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}; \cref{rem:islocallytrivial_off_path}
    relates \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) and \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\). Nothing
    on the critical path depends on these.
  \item \cref{prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid} uses
    \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
          lem:scheme_modules_tensorobjisoofiso,
          lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
          lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
          lem:tensorobj_right_unitor,
          lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
          def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}.
  \item \cref{rem:onproduct_repoint} uses
    \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid}.
  \item \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct} uses
    \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid,%
          lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}.
  \item \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj} uses
    \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct} and implicitly
    \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} from
    \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}.
  \item \cref{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj} uses
    \cref{prop:tensorobj_class_commmonoid,

          lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}; the last two are in
    \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} (A.1.b) and
    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c) respectively.
\end{itemize}
All \(\uses\) targets resolve either in this chapter, in
