1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the Lean carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
88	available; \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and the structure-sheaf unit are present as
89	scheme-level objects. Rather than the geometric local-triviality route, the
90	group law is built on \(\otimes\)-\emph{invertibility}: an object is a line
91	object when it admits a tensor inverse, \(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong
92	\mathcal{O}_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), exactly Mathlib's
93	\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom. Under this predicate requirement (3)
94	becomes the three monoidal \emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) ---
95	associator, unitors and braiding --- each obtained cheaply as
96	\(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) of the corresponding presheaf-level
97	isomorphism, while the inverse is carried by the predicate itself. These
98	assemble the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units group is
99	the Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The geometric
100	compatibility isomorphism \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\)
101	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and any separate tensor-inverse lemma are
102	\emph{off} this critical path. The full symmetric monoidal category on
103	\emph{all} quasi-coherent modules is also \emph{not} required; see
104	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
105	
106	\section{Mathlib API survey}
107	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
108	
109	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
110	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
111	
112	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
113	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
114	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
115	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
116	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
117	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
118	sectionwise tensor product
119	\[
120	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
121	\]
122	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
123	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
124	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
125	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
126	
127	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
128	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
129	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
130	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
131	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
132	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
133	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
134	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
135	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
136	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
137	tensor-product operation
138	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
139	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
140	
141	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
142	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
143	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
144	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
145	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
146	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\). The data this operation must supply for the group law are the monoidal
147	\emph{coherence isomorphisms} of \(\otimes_X\) --- the associator
148	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), the left/right unitors
149	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}) and the braiding
150	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) --- each obtained by the cheap pattern
151	\(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) applied to the corresponding presheaf-level
152	isomorphism on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\), with no recourse to the
153	abstract \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) adjoint. Together with the
154	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
155	(which carries the inverse existentially) these assemble the commutative monoid
156	of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units group
157	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}). The project does \emph{not} require a
158	global \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance on all quasi-coherent
159	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure:
160	monoidal coherence beyond these three isomorphisms is never consumed by a group
161	law on iso-classes (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}). The
162	open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
163	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is \emph{not} on this critical path and is
164	retained only as an optional supplement.
165	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
166	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
167	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
168	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
169	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
170	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
171	independently of any upstream timing.
172	
173	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
174	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
175	
176	\begin{definition}
177	
178	\leanok
179	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
180	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
181	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
182	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
183	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
184	  \[
185	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
186	  \]
187	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
188	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
189	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
190	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
191	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
192	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
193	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
194	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
195	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
196	  canonical compatibility
197	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
198	     \;\cong\;
199	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
200	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
201	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
202	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
203	
204	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
205	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
206	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
207	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
208	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
209	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
210	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
211	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
212	\end{definition}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}
215	
216	\leanok
217	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
218	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
219	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
220	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
221	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
222	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
223	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
224	  \[
225	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
226	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
227	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
228	  \]
229	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
230	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
231	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
232	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
233	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
234	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
235	  \[
236	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
237	    \qquad
238	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
239	  \]
240	  the associator
241	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
242	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
243	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
244	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they have no current Lean
245	  declaration and are off the critical path, recorded in
246	  \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
247	\end{lemma}
248	
249	\begin{proof}
250	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
251	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
252	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
253	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
254	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
255	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
256	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
257	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
258	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
259	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
260	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
261	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
262	\end{proof}
263	
264	\begin{remark}
265	[The full monoidal category on all modules is off the critical path]
266	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
267	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
268	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
269	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
270	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
271	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
272	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
273	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This chapter deliberately does
274	  \emph{not} pursue that, and the relative Picard group law does \emph{not}
275	  depend on it.
276	
277	  The reason is that the relative Picard group law of
278	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on \emph{isomorphism
279	  classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
280	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
281	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
282	  coherence data of a monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
283	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never consumed by the group
284	  law. Conversely, building the global \(\MonoidalCategory\) on all
285	  quasi-coherent modules would route through the localization-monoidal
286	  transport \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal}\), which demands a
287	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\,W\) instance for the
288	  sheafification-localizing morphism property \(W\); the
289	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) stability \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\)
290	  of that property, for an \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\), is provable only
291	  through a \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) ---
292	  absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build. That cost
293	  buys coherence the group law never uses.
294	
295	  The group law needs only the four existence-of-iso lemmas of
296	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}, each assembled from the
297	  restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}. We
298	  therefore record the global monoidal category as explicitly \emph{off the
299	  critical path} and build the group law directly on the line-bundle
300	  subcategory. This mirrors Mathlib's own Picard group
301	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
302	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
303	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\) over
304	  a fixed ring and needs no sheafification, no localization, and no
305	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\).
306	\end{remark}
307	
308	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
309	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
310	
311	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
312	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
313	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
314	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
315	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section first isolates the one
316	genuinely-hard ingredient --- the restriction-compatibility
317	isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} --- and then records the
318	\emph{four existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity,
319	unit, commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law
320	consumes, each assembled from that ingredient on a common trivialising
321	cover, together with their restriction to the
322	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype and the interaction with the
323	pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). These four facts (\emph{not} a
324	\(\MonoidalCategory\) instance) are exactly the group-law data; cf.\
325	\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
326	
327	\begin{lemma}
328	
329	\leanok
330	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
331	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
332	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
333	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
334	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
335	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
336	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
337	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
338	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
339	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
340	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
341	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
342	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
343	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
344	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
345	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
346	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
347	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
348	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
349	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
350	  \[
351	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
352	      \;\;:=\;\;
353	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
354	  \]
355	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
356	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
357	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
358	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
359	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
360	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
361	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
362	\end{lemma}
363	
364	\begin{proof}
365	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
366	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
367	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
368	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
369	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
370	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
371	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
372	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
373	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
374	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
375	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
376	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
377	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
378	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
379	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
380	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
381	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
382	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
383	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
384	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
385	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
386	
387	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
388	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
389	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
390	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
391	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
392	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
393	\end{proof}
394	
395	\begin{lemma}
396	
397	\leanok
398	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
399	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
400	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
401	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
402	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
403	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
404	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
405	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
406	  morphism
407	  \[
408	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
409	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
410	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
411	  \]
412	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
413	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
414	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
415	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
416	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
417	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products. The intended Lean
418	  target is
419	  \(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj\_restrict\_iso}\).
420	\end{lemma}
421	
422	\begin{proof}
423	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
424	  % NOTE: iter-208 prover (Lane TS, Route A) DISPROVED Step 3 as written. The
425	  % "sectionwise unfolding of PresheafOfModules.pullback varphi (~30--60 lines)"
426	  % does NOT exist: that functor is the opaque abstract left adjoint of
427	  % pushforward and has no sectionwise formula (the proof text below even admits
428	  % the opacity, then assumes an unfolding anyway). The genuine residual needs
429	  % TWO project-side, Mathlib-ABSENT ingredients (~200--300 LOC, mathlib-build
430	  % scale): (H1) a PRESHEAF-level iso `pushforward beta ~= pullback varphi` via
431	  % Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq from a presheaf-level pushforward adjunction
432	  % (needs presheaf pushforwardNatTrans + pushforwardCongr; only SHEAF versions
433	  % exist), and (H2) strong-monoidal `restrictScalars` along the ring iso
434	  % f.appIso (upgrade the file's lax `restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal` to strong;
435	  % bottoms out in the absent "restrictScalars along a ring iso is strong
436	  % monoidal"). Steps 1--2 (restrictFunctorIsoPullback, sheafificationCompPullback,
437	  % strip outer sheafification via mapIso) ARE formalized and GREEN. Step 3 and
438	  % the closing "30--60 line helper" claim must be rewritten to the H1/H2
439	  % decomposition before this is re-attempted. See
440	  % informal/tensorObj_restrict_iso.md and task_results for the corrected route.
441	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
442	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
443	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
444	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
445	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
446	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
447	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
448	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
449	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
450	  isomorphism}
451	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
452	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
453	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
454	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
455	  therefore extension of scalars along
456	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
457	
458	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
459	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
460	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
461	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
462	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
463	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
464	  the abstract pullback functor.
465	
466	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
467	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
468	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
469	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
470	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
471	   \;\cong\;
472	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
473	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
474	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
475	  comparison
476	  \[
477	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
478	      \;\longrightarrow\;
479	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
480	      \;\otimes\;
481	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
482	  \]
483	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
484	
485	  \emph{Step 3 (off the critical path).} Completing this comparison at the
486	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise description of the abstract functor
487	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
488	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the
489	  pinned commit. The genuine residual decomposes into two Mathlib-absent
490	  presheaf-level ingredients --- a presheaf-level identification
491	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) via
492	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (needing a presheaf-level pushforward
493	  adjunction, only the sheaf version of which exists), and a strong-monoidal
494	  upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the ring isomorphism
495	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\). This lemma is therefore \emph{not} on the critical path
496	  for the relative Picard group law: the group law is built from
497	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) and the
498	  coherence isomorphisms
499	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
500	  none of which consume restriction-compatibility. The statement is retained
501	  only as an optional supplement; its full proof is deferred.
502	\end{proof}

504	\begin{lemma}
505	
506	\leanok
507	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
508	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
510	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
511	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
512	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
513	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
514	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
515	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
516	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
517	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
518	\end{lemma}
519	
520	\begin{proof}
521	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
522	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
523	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
524	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
525	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
526	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
527	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
528	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
529	\end{proof}

531	\begin{lemma}
532	[Associator for $\otimes_X$]
533	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
534	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
535	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
536	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
537	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. There is a natural isomorphism
538	  \[
539	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
540	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
541	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
542	  \]
543	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). No line-bundle, local-freeness or
544	  flatness hypothesis is required.
545	\end{lemma}
546	
547	\begin{proof}
548	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
549	  The substrate \(\otimes_X\) is, by \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}, the
550	  sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
551	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\). The presheaf category
552	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) is monoidal
553	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}), so it carries
554	  an associator natural isomorphism \(\alpha\) at the presheaf level. Applying
555	  the sheafification functor to \(\alpha\) --- the cheap pattern
556	  \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\alpha)\), exactly the shape of the existing
557	  structure-sheaf-unit comparison and of \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\) --- and
558	  composing with the comparison identifying iterated sheafified tensor products
559	  with the sheafification of the iterated presheaf tensor product yields the
560	  asserted isomorphism. The construction uses only the monoidal coherence data
561	  already present on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\); it invokes neither
562	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\) nor any open-immersion
563	  restriction-compatibility.
564	\end{proof}

568	  \label{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
569	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor, AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor}
570	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

572	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. With
573	  \(\mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) the
574	  structure-sheaf unit object, there are natural isomorphisms
575	  \[
576	    \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; M,
577	    \qquad

579	  \]

585	  the left and right unitors \(\lambda, \rho\) at the presheaf level. Their
586	  sheafifications \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\lambda)\),
587	  \(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}(\rho)\), composed with the sheafification
588	  counit identifying the sheafified structure-sheaf presheaf with
589	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (the step already used in the structure-sheaf-unit
590	  comparison \(\mathtt{tensorObj\_unit\_iso}\) in the substrate file), give the
591	  two isomorphisms. As with the associator the route is the cheap
592	  \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern and uses no abstract pullback.
593	\end{proof}

601	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. There is a natural isomorphism

604	  \]

611	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}). Its

616	\begin{lemma}
617	
618	\leanok
619	[Inverse of an invertible module]
620	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
621	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_tensorObj_inverse}
622	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
623	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
624	  % NOTE: off the critical path for the relative Picard group law. The group
625	  % law uses the tensor-invertibility predicate IsInvertible
626	  % (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible), which carries the inverse existentially,
627	  % so no separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is required. Retained only
628	  % as a supplement on the geometric line-bundle subcategory.
629	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
630	  bundle. The dual
631	  \[
632	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
633	  \]
634	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
635	  isomorphism
636	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
637	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
638	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
639	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
640	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
641	\end{lemma}
642	
643	\begin{proof}
644	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
645	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
646	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
647	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
648	  The contraction map
649	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
650	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
651	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
652	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
653	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
654	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
655	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
656	\end{proof}

717	\begin{lemma}
718	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
719	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
720	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}

722	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
723	  functor
724	  \[
725	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
726	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
727	  \]
728	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
729	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
730	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
731	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
732	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
733	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
734	  canonical isomorphism
735	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
736	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
737	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
738	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
739	\end{lemma}
740	
741	\begin{proof}
742	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
743	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
744	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
745	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
746	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
747	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
748	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
749	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
750	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
751	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
752	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
753	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
754	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
755	  same cover.
756	\end{proof}

758	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
759	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
760	
761	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
762	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
763	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
764	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
765	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
766	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
767	design template. The inverse of an invertible object is carried by the predicate
768	itself, so neither the open-immersion restriction-compatibility isomorphism
769	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a separate tensor-inverse lemma is on the
770	critical path.
771	
772	\begin{definition}[\(\otimes\)-invertibility of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
773	  \label{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
774	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible}
775	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
776	  % SOURCE: [Stacks], Tag 01HK (invertible modules) (verbatim text not yet
777	  % retrieved --- reference-retriever dispatch required before a verbatim
778	  % SOURCE QUOTE block may be added here).
779	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
780	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
781	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
782	  \[
783	    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
784	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
785	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
786	  \]
787	  i.e.\ \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M) :\equiv \exists\, N,\
788	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
789	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
790	  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
791	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
792	  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
793	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
794	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
795	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
796	  \(\otimes_X\).
797	\end{definition}
798	
799	\begin{lemma}[The commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes and its units]
800	  \label{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}
801	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}
802	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
803	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,

806	  % retrieved --- reference-retriever dispatch required before a verbatim

812	  because \(\otimes_X\) is a bifunctor

814	  \(M \cong M'\) to \(M \otimes_X N \cong M' \otimes_X N\) and symmetrically);

867	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
868	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.

870	\begin{theorem}

872	\leanok

886	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),

907	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,

909	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
910	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
911	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
912	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
913	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
914	  \[
915	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
916	  \]
917	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
918	  abelian-group structure with addition
919	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
920	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
921	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
922	  inverse
923	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
924	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
925	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
926	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
927	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
928	  map
929	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
930	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
931	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
932	\end{theorem}
933	
934	\begin{proof}
935	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
936	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
937	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
938	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
939	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
940	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
941	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
942	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
943	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
944	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
945	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
946	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
947	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
948	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
949	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
950	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
951	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
952	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
953	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
954	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
955	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
956	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
957	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
958	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
959	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
960	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
961	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
962	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
963	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
964	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
965	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
966	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
967	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
968	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
969	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
970	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
971	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
972	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
973	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
974	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
975	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
976	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
977	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
978	
979	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
980	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
981	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
982	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
983	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
984	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
985	
986	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
987	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
988	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
989	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
990	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
991	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
992	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
993	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
994	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
995	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
996	\end{proof}
997

Estimate: the unitors and the braiding are \(\approx 15\) LOC each (the bare
\(\mathtt{sheafification.mapIso}\) pattern); the \(\otimes\)-invertibility
predicate is a few lines. The associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}) is the
one exception: because it nests a sheafification inside the presheaf tensor on
both sides, it additionally requires the \emph{sheafification--tensor absorption}
isomorphism
\(\mathtt{sheafification}(\mathtt{sheafification}(A).\mathtt{val} \otimes^{p} B)
\cong \mathtt{sheafification}(A \otimes^{p} B)\) (whiskering-stability of the
sheafification localizer for an arbitrary module), the same stability flagged in
\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}; it should be scheduled as a separate
helper ahead of the consumer. Total for Piece 2: roughly \(50\)--\(70\) LOC if the
absorption iso is cheap, more if it must be built from scratch. This is still
materially lighter than a full coherence-axiom discharge: no
pentagon/triangle/hexagon identity is proved, and the open-immersion
restriction-compatibility isomorphism is \emph{not} needed.

input.old_string:
[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
  % NOTE: the Lean target `tensorObjOnProduct` provides ONLY the
  % operation-closure on the subtype (that the tensor product of two
  % `LineBundle.OnProduct` objects is again one). The remaining group-law
  % data stated in this block --- unit-membership of O_{C×T}, the
  % dual/inverse being a two-sided tensor inverse, and the
  % associativity/unit/commutativity existence-of-iso facts --- are SEPARATE
  % items, NOT delivered by `tensorObjOnProduct`. Each is blocked on
  % \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
  \[
    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
    \;\;=\;\;
    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
  \]
  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
  Direct from
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
  associativity, unit and commutativity isomorphisms of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
  are isomorphisms of line bundles, hence restrict to the subtype;
  together with the inverse of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
  they equip the isomorphism classes of
  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with the full
  group-law data (associativity, unit, commutativity, inverse).
\end{proof}
input.new_string:
[Closure of \(\otimes\) on the \(\otimes\)-invertible carrier
  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
  % NOTE: the downstream carrier `LineBundle.OnProduct` (in
  % chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback) is RE-INSTANTIATED on the IsInvertible
  % predicate (def:scheme_modules_isinvertible), the iter-174 documented intent
  % ("OnProduct pairs a carrier with an IsInvertible witness"). The Lean target
  % `tensorObjOnProduct` provides the operation-closure on this subtype. The
  % geometric predicate IsLocallyTrivial is retained SEPARATELY and connected to
  % IsInvertible only off the critical path (see the Out-of-scope section). This
  % chapter only references the planned re-point so its own statements are
  % self-consistent; the re-instantiation itself lives in
  % chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback and is NOT edited here.
  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes. Under the planned re-point of
  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, the carrier is the
  \(\otimes\)-invertible subtype
  \[
    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
    \;\;=\;\;
    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\},
  \]
  the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate of
  \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}. The bifunctor
  \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to this subtype:
  if \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\) and \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M'\) then
  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes_{C \times_S T} M')\); the unit
  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\) is invertible; and every invertible module
  carries its tensor inverse. Hence the isomorphism classes of the subtype form
  the units group of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} on
  \(C \times_S T\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
  Closure under \(\otimes\) is the units-closed-under-multiplication statement of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}: if \(M \otimes N \cong \mathcal{O}\)
  and \(M' \otimes N' \cong \mathcal{O}\), then
  \((M \otimes M') \otimes (N \otimes N') \cong \mathcal{O}\) by the coherence
  isomorphisms, so \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes M')\) with witness
  \(N \otimes N'\). The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\) is invertible with
  witness itself (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the tensor inverse is
  carried by the predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). Thus the
  subtype is a submonoid all of whose elements are units --- the abelian group
  of iso-classes that is the units group of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} restricted to \(C \times_S T\).
\end{proof}

Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
  \[
    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
    \;\;=\;\;
    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
  \]
  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible,
        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
  Direct from
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
  associativity, unit and commutativity isomorphisms of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
  are isomorphisms of line bundles, hence restrict to the subtype;
  together with the inverse of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
  they equip the isomorphism classes of
  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with the full
  group-law data (associativity, unit, commutativity, inverse).
\end{proof}
input.new_string:
Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes. Under the planned re-point of
  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, the carrier is the
  \(\otimes\)-invertible subtype
  \[
    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
    \;\;=\;\;
    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\}
  \]
  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}. The bifunctor
  \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to this subtype:
  if \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\) and \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M'\) then
  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes_{C \times_S T} M')\); the unit
  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\) is invertible; and every invertible module
  carries its own tensor inverse. Hence the isomorphism classes of the subtype
  form the units group of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} on
  \(C \times_S T\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
  Closure under \(\otimes\) is the units-closed-under-multiplication statement of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}: if \(M \otimes N \cong \mathcal{O}\)
  and \(M' \otimes N' \cong \mathcal{O}\), then
  \((M \otimes M') \otimes (N \otimes N') \cong \mathcal{O}\) by the coherence
  isomorphisms, so \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M \otimes M')\) with witness
  \(N \otimes N'\). The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\) is invertible with
  witness itself (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the tensor inverse is
  carried by the predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). Thus the
  subtype is a submonoid all of whose elements are units --- the abelian group
  of iso-classes that is the units group of
  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} restricted to \(C \times_S T\).
\end{proof}

When I first read the chapter it was the old (1148-line) version with the
geometric-local-triviality critical path. While I was preparing the rewrite, the
file was repeatedly modified out from under me by another process (the on-disk
file grew to 1206 then 1303 lines, and `git diff HEAD` was empty at one point) —

API-survey "gap"). I therefore did **not** do a full overwrite (it would have
clobbered that work and lost concurrent improvements). Instead I completed the
items the concurrent editor left unfinished or got wrong, by surgical edits.
**Flag for plan agent:** confirm only one writer was assigned to this chapter —
concurrent edits to one `.tex` are a race hazard.

## Changes Made (my surgical edits)
- **Filled the `IsInvertible` citation** (`def:scheme_modules_isinvertible`):
  the block had a placeholder `% SOURCE: [Stacks], Tag 01HK ... (verbatim text
  not yet retrieved)`. **The tag was wrong** (01HK is "closed immersion of
  locally ringed spaces", nothing to do with invertibility). Replaced with the
  verbatim Stacks **Tag 01CR** text — Definition 01CS (`definition-invertible`)
  + Lemma 0B8K (`lemma-invertible`, the `∃N, L⊗N≅𝒪` characterisation) — copied

  a visible `\textit{Source: …}` line.

  placeholder `% SOURCE: [Stacks], Tag 01CR ... (verbatim text not yet
  retrieved)` replaced with the verbatim **Tag 01CR** text — Definition 01CX
  (`definition-pic`, "Picard group = abelian group of iso-classes under ⊗") +
  Lemma `lemma-constructions-invertible` (⊗ of invertibles is invertible; dual
  is a tensor inverse) — from `references/stacks-modules.tex` L4200–L4213 and
  L4350–L4357, plus a visible `\textit{Source: …}` line.
- **Re-pointed `OnProduct` lift lemma** (`lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct`,
  directive item 5): the NOTE had already been updated to the IsInvertible
  re-point by the concurrent editor, but the **statement + proof still described

- **The directive's "confirmed on disk" claim was false.** `references/01CR.md`
  and `references/01HK.md` did **not** exist; I dispatched a reference-retriever

- **Two directive tag misidentifications** (now corrected in the chapter):
  - Tag **01CR** is in the **Modules on Ringed Spaces** chapter (§17.25
    "Invertible modules"), *not* the Divisors chapter. Its definition is 01CS,
    the `∃N` characterisation is **0B8K**, and the Picard group is **01CX**.

    "closed immersion of locally ringed spaces". The directive's instruction to
    "quote 01HK for the IsInvertible predicate" would have produced a wrong
    citation; I used 01CS/0B8K instead.
- The concurrent-editing situation (above) should be resolved before the next

  remain as off-path supplements with `\leanok` (green Lean decls). They are no
  longer on the group-law `\uses` chain; left in place per directive (do not
  delete validated helpers).

## Strategy-modifying findings
**The associator coherence iso is NOT the cheap ~15-LOC `sheafification.mapIso`
pattern the directive (item 2) assumed; it relocates — does not eliminate — the
Mathlib gap.**

- `tensorObj M N := sheafification(ptensor(M.val, N.val))`. Hence
  `tensorObj (tensorObj M N) P = sheafification(ptensor(sheafification(ptensor M.val N.val).val, P.val))`
  — the associator nests a sheafification **inside** the presheaf tensor on both
  sides. The unitors and braiding do **not** nest (one factor is the unit, or the
  two sides are the same tensor reordered), so for them the bare
  `sheafification.mapIso(λ/ρ/β)` pattern genuinely works (~15 LOC, as the
  directive says, matching the existing green `tensorObj_unit_iso`).
- To transport the presheaf associator `α`, one additionally needs the
  **sheafification–tensor absorption** iso
  `sheafification(ptensor(sheafification(A).val, B)) ≅ sheafification(ptensor(A,B))`
  (and its mirror): the sheafification unit `η_A` must become an iso after
  whiskering by an arbitrary module `B` and re-sheafifying.
- That is **exactly** the whiskering-stability `W g ⇒ W(F ◁ g)` for arbitrary
  `F` which the chapter's own `rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path` states is
  "provable only through a `MonoidalClosed(PresheafOfModules R)` — absent from
  Mathlib at the pinned commit, and a multi-file build." So the ⊗-invertibility
  pivot removes the `tensorObj_restrict_iso` / `PresheafOfModules.pullback`
  obstruction for the unitors/braiding and the inverse (genuine wins), but the
  **associator still bottoms out in the same absent infrastructure**, because the
  CommMonoid law must hold for *arbitrary* `M,N,P` (it is the monoid on iso-classes
  of ALL of `X.Modules`, units = invertibles, per the directive's
  `CommRing.Pic = Units(Skeleton(ModuleCat R))` template — but `ModuleCat R` has a
  genuine `MonoidalCategory` instance, which `X.Modules` lacks).
- I have written the chapter per the directive's construction (the design is
  sound), with the associator block carrying an explicit `% NOTE` and the LOC
  estimate flagging the absorption iso as the one piece that may need building
  first. **Recommendation:** before pushing a prover at `lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso`
  / the CommMonoid assembly, re-consult the mathlib-analogist on whether the
  absorption iso `sheafification(ptensor(sheafification A, B)) ≅
  sheafification(ptensor(A,B))` is available at `b80f227` as an individual iso
  (e.g. from sheafification being a reflective/⊗-localization) **without** the
  full `MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal W` / `MonoidalClosed` build. If it is not,
  STRATEGY.md should record that the associator (hence the CommMonoid on all
  iso-classes) is a residual gap, and consider building the group law only on the
  invertible (locally-free rank-1) subcategory where local trivialisations may
  give the associator directly.
